projects.title: S1napse
projects.description: Built to see my own ACC laps, S1napse grew into a full sim + real-racing telemetry suite — self-building track maps, per-corner coaching, and an ELM327 bridge that brings the same tools to a real car.
projects.details:
**I'm a sim racer**, and every telemetry tool I tried was either locked behind a subscription, tied to one specific sim, or so cluttered I couldn't actually read it mid-lap. The ones that worked for ACC didn't work for iRacing, nothing touched real cars, and the analysis tools were either too shallow or built for engineers, not drivers. So I built the thing I actually wanted to use.

**S1napse is a Python desktop app** (PySide6) that talks to six different telemetry sources behind one unified reader interface: ACC, AC, iRacing, Le Mans Ultimate, and rFactor 2 over shared memory or UDP, plus real cars through an ELM327 OBD-II adapter. On top of that sits a math channel engine with its own formula parser, a corner detector and trail-braking analyzer that generates coaching insights after each session, a tyre and strategy module, and a track map that builds itself from the laps you drive. Ten tabs in total, split between dense live HUDs you can read at 200 km/h and roomier analysis views for after the session.

I designed and shipped the whole thing end to end, from the protocol-level readers up through the UI system, packaging, and a Windows installer. **It's free, actively developed, and the people using it are people like me.**

Stack: Python, PySide6/Qt, NumPy, shared memory + UDP, PyInstaller.

projects.title: bigfiles
projects.description: Find the big files, old files, and duplicates clogging your computer and reclaim gigabytes you forgot you had.
projects.details:
**The problem.** Every few months my laptop fills up and I have no idea why. Finder's "Storage" tab gives vague categories. du spits out walls of numbers. The GUI cleaners want a subscription to tell me my Downloads folder is big. None of them answer the actual question: what's taking up space, and is it safe to delete?

**How it started.** I was 12GB from full, again, and decided to just build the tool I wanted. Something that scans fast, groups files by what they actually are (videos, installers, caches, old downloads), flags stuff I haven't touched in years, and catches the duplicate copies I keep making of the same folder. No GUI, no subscription, no telemetry — just a clear answer in one command.

**The result. **bigfiles scans my entire home directory in seconds, shows me exactly where the weight is, surfaces duplicates I didn't know I had, and lets me clean them up interactively without ever leaving the terminal. The first time I ran it on my own machine, it found 40GB of forgotten video exports and duplicate project folders. I've shipped it to crates.io so anyone else who's tired of guessing can use it too.

projects.title: Entroscope
projects.description: The definitive entropy toolkit for time series data — seven entropy measures behind one consistent interface that works directly on pandas Series and numpy arrays.
projects.details:
**The problem.** While building NextOnMenu I noticed something: when a food's regional search interest started trending, the Shannon entropy of that signal would quietly fall *before* it went mainstream. Falling entropy meant the noise was becoming structure. It was a genuinely useful early signal — but every time I wanted it, I rewrote the same histogram-and-log boilerplate by hand. And Shannon is only one of a dozen entropy measures; the moment I wanted permutation or spectral entropy, I was back to copying formulas off papers and second-guessing the math.

**How it started.** I pulled that scattered code out of NextOnMenu and decided to write it once, properly. The goal was a library where every entropy measure you'd ever reach for lives behind the same tiny interface — `compute`, `rolling`, `delta`, `normalized`, `plot` — so switching from Shannon to permutation to spectral entropy is a one-word change, not a rewrite. It had to take a pandas Series and hand you back a Series with its index intact, or take a numpy array and hand back an array. No ceremony, no per-measure quirks.

**The result.** entroscope ships seven entropy measures — Shannon, permutation, sample, approximate, spectral, differential, and multiscale — plus transfer entropy for directional information flow between two signals and KL/JS divergence for distribution drift. Every measure has rolling and delta variants and built-in matplotlib plotting, and there's a visualization module to overlay measures, render a full dashboard, or flag sharp entropy drops for regime-change detection. The correctness of the hard cases is validated against closed-form analytic baselines, not just eyeballed. It's tested across Python 3.9 through 3.14 with a 90% coverage gate and published to PyPI, so `pip install entroscope` gets anyone the one-liner I wished I'd had.

projects.title: Sim.ula
projects.description: Cities are living experiments, but sim.ula believes the lab shouldn't be the streets themselves.
projects.details:
**The Challenge: High-Stakes Urban Planning**
In highly dense urban environments, policy decisions cannot afford to be experimental. In regions where traffic congestion drains 3.5 billion pesos daily and population density reaches up to 40,000 people per square kilometer, building software just for the sake of building is a liability. Local government units need a way to validate interventions before deployment. A hallucinated or poorly translated recommendation in this environment doesn't just fail; it risks multi-million peso disasters.

**The Solution: sim.ula**
sim.ula is an AI-driven urban policy simulation platform designed to act as a rigorous testing ground—a lab for living cities. Born out of an intensive 24-hour hackathon and hours of deliberate, screen-off ideological framing, this platform provides city planners with scientifically grounded, scalable, and highly usable blueprints for high-stakes decision-making.

Instead of rushing to write code, the foundation of sim.ula was built on ensuring real-world applicability. The system was piloted using a curated vector database of 94 city policy precedents from across Southeast Asia *(evaluating interventions from cities like Singapore and Jakarta)*, specifically tailored and contextualized for the unique infrastructural demands of Quezon City.

**Technical Architecture**
The engineering architecture of sim.ula is built to guarantee precision, contextual relevance, and strict adherence to factual civic data:

- GraphRAG with Bidirectional CTEs: Maps the complex, non-linear relationships between specific urban interventions and their cascading long-term socio-economic effects.

- Transferability Chains: A custom logic layer that calculates the "contextual distance" between cities. This ensures that an infrastructure policy successful in Jakarta is rigorously evaluated for its actual applicability to a new target city before being recommended.

- DeBERTa NLI Hallucination Guard: A strict validation layer designed specifically for the high-stakes nature of public policy, actively preventing AI hallucinations from entering the municipal decision-making pipeline.

- Scalable Infrastructure: Engineered utilizing Ollama for efficient, localized model execution and routed through Cloudflare to ensure secure, low-latency access for end-users.

**The Impact**
Awarded 3rd Place Overall at its debuting hackathon (built alongside Charles Joshua Uy and Prince Rivera). Beyond the podium placement, sim.ula represents a fundamental shift in how civic technology should be built: prioritizing deep, analytical problem-solving and architectural integrity over rapid, blind coding. It is a scalable tool that successfully turns speculative "what ifs" into concrete, evidence-based municipal action.

projects.title: One Big Match
projects.description: Breaking the ice in a sea of new faces shouldn’t be a matter of chance.
projects.details:
Blending elements of dating apps and party games, One Big Match is designed for event-based social connection. It takes the headache out of "who should pair with who" by smart-matching people based on common traits or vibes, sparking connections that feel natural from the very first hello.

**The Problem**
Gatherings can easily fall flat without a natural way to break the ice. Whether organizing a networking mixer, a team-building session, or a club meetup, event planners need a tool to help people connect faster and with less awkwardness.

**The Solution**
We handle the matching; you bring the people. OBM acts as a social algorithm built for groups, taking care of the pairing so organizers can focus purely on the experience.

**Core Features**
Interest & Character-Based Matching: Forms groups of people based on their shared interests and personality traits to host smoother-flowing conversations.

- Customizable Host Controls: Hosts have full control to change the number of people per group and switch between randomized or similarity-based matching methods.

- Frictionless Access: No downloads or logins required for attendees—they simply scan a QR code or tap a link to join the room.

- Privacy by Design: Collects only essential information (name, email, and basic profile details). Users can update or delete their profiles and event history at any time.

**How It Works**
The Attendee Experience: Users quickly sign up with Google, upload their snazziest picture, and complete a short personality quiz to let their personality shine.

The Host Experience: Hosts create an event, set the max participants, and share the room code. Once everyone is in, the host hits "Start Matching," and the app takes care of the rest.

projects.title: TedX
projects.description: Ideas worth spreading deserve a digital stage built to match their ambition.
projects.details:
**Project Overview**
Designed and developed the official event website to translate the abstract theme of "Momentum" into an engaging, interactive digital experience for the Ateneo community. Serving as the central touchpoint for the 2026 conference, the platform guides visitors seamlessly from initial discovery down to final attendee registration.

**Core Features**
- Thematic UI/UX: Integrated the concept of "Momentum" through scroll-driven interactions that physically mirror the theme of movement.

- Interactive Reveals: Built engaging "open the doors" interface elements to creatively introduce the speaker lineup.

- Seamless Registration: Developed a frictionless, dynamic ticketing flow tailored to securely capture data for various participant tiers.

- Centralized Hub: Housed all critical conference logistics in one accessible space, including responsive agendas, integrated venue mapping, and sponsor highlights.

**The Impact**
The platform successfully anchored the digital footprint for the conference, transforming a complex onboarding process into a smooth user journey and delivering a premium, scalable web presence worthy of the TEDx brand.

projects.title: Sigla
projects.description: The first step of every Atenean’s journey begins long before they set foot on campus;it starts with a single click.
projects.details:
Developed a web-based utility that transforms standard, text-heavy college class schedules into clean, customized mobile and desktop wallpapers. Designed specifically for students, this tool eliminates the friction of constantly logging into academic portals or digging through photo galleries just to check the time and room number for their next class.

**Core Features**
- Dynamic Grid Layouts: Automatically parses inputted class data (subject, time, room, professor) and seamlessly organizes it into an optimized, highly readable visual grid.

- Aesthetic Customization: Allows users to personalize their generated schedules with custom color palettes, background images, and typography to match their specific vibe.

- Instant High-Res Export: Utilizes client-side rendering to instantly convert the schedule layout into a high-resolution PNG/JPEG file, perfectly scaled for modern mobile lock screens or desktop displays.

- Frictionless UI: Built with an intuitive, no-clutter interface that makes data entry, customization, and downloading a fast, one-stop process.

**The Impact**
Transformed a mundane administrative necessity into a practical, visually pleasing design experience. The generator provides students with a fast, reliable, and highly personalized way to keep their daily academic routines accessible right from their lock screens.
